questions.question: Explain why an arithmetic progression represents linear growth.
questions.answer: Because the difference between consecutive terms remains constant.

questions.question: Why does an AP with negative common difference decrease?
questions.answer: Because each term is obtained by subtracting a fixed value.

questions.question: Explain why an AP with d = 0 remains constant.
questions.answer: Because no change occurs between consecutive terms.

questions.question: Why does the graph of an AP form a straight line?
questions.answer: Because the change between terms is uniform.

questions.question: Explain why the nth term of an AP is linear in n.
questions.answer: Because it is expressed as a + (n − 1)d.

questions.question: Why is the first term important in an AP?
questions.answer: Because all other terms depend on the first term.

questions.question: Explain why common difference controls the rate of change.
questions.answer: Because it determines how much each term increases or decreases.

questions.question: Why can an AP have fractional common difference?
questions.answer: Because the common difference can be any real number.

questions.question: Explain why AP is suitable for modelling salary increments.
questions.answer: Because increments are usually uniform each year.

questions.question: Why is AP not suitable for compound interest modelling?
questions.answer: Because compound interest grows exponentially, not linearly.

questions.question: Explain why an infinite AP does not have a last term.
questions.answer: Because the sequence continues endlessly.

questions.question: Why is the nth term formula not applicable to non-AP sequences?
questions.answer: Because non-AP sequences do not have constant difference.

questions.question: Explain why AP is a special type of sequence.
questions.answer: Because it has a constant difference between terms.

questions.question: Why does changing d change the slope of the AP graph?
questions.answer: Because d represents the rate of increase or decrease.

questions.question: Explain why AP models uniform motion.
questions.answer: Because distance changes uniformly with time.

questions.question: Why does an AP with a negative first term become positive later?
questions.answer: Because repeated addition of a positive common difference increases terms.

questions.question: Explain why AP cannot model exponential growth.
questions.answer: Because exponential growth does not have constant differences.

questions.question: Why is understanding AP important in mathematics?
questions.answer: Because it forms the basis for sequences and series.

questions.question: Explain why the difference between consecutive terms remains same in AP.
questions.answer: Because of the definition of arithmetic progression.

questions.question: Why does AP always show a definite pattern?
questions.answer: Because the common difference is fixed.

questions.question: Explain why AP is useful in real-life modelling.
questions.answer: Because many real-life situations involve uniform change.

questions.question: Why does an AP with d = 1 represent natural numbers?
questions.answer: Because each term increases by one.

questions.question: Why does an AP of even numbers increase by 2?
questions.answer: Because each even number differs from the next by 2.

questions.question: Explain why AP is not suitable for population growth modelling.
questions.answer: Because population growth is usually exponential.

questions.question: Explain the importance of arithmetic progression in mathematics.
questions.answer: Because it helps in understanding linear sequences and series.
